question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 310km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 21 units of relief supply box (60kg each) and 10 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 165km waypoint, 12 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 111 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 56 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 56min, 2nd: 88min, 3rd: 120min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

  Congestion: 9:00 AM~3:00 PM, Zone B -33%
  Mid-journey delivery: 165km, relief supply box x12
  Cumulative fatigue: +32min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 31, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 27, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 14}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 111, 'duration_minutes': 56, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 11}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 300, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 24, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 36}}

[STEP 2] Cargo regulation: 1440kg > 300kg -> Zone A -24%(20.52), Zone B -36%(8.96)

  -> 11.00km / 20.52km/h = 0.5361h

  -> 9.52km / 20.52km/h = 0.4639h

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 160km waypoint, 13 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

[STEP 0] Initial conditions

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 300km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 22 units of construction material (80kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (58kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 99km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 73 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 73min, 2nd: 104min, 3rd: 135min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 315km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (59kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (32kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 288km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 105 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 85 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 85min, 2nd: 121min, 3rd: 157min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=315km, boat still-water speed=28km/h, departure=9:00 AM
  Current: 8km/h (downstream in Zone B)
  Cargo: 59kg construction materialx11 + 32kg relief supply boxx4
  Congestion: 10:00 AM~3:00 PM, Zone B -35%
  Mid-journey delivery: 288km, construction material x4
  Cumulative fatigue: +36min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 40, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 23, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 14}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 105, 'duration_minutes': 85, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 9}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 400, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 25, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 34}}
[STEP 1] Cargo weight: (59x11)+(32x4)=777kg

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 338km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (77kg each) and 9 units of water barrel (25kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 99km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 80 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 80min, 2nd: 114min, 3rd: 148min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 306km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (30kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (11kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 36km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.

From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 135km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 85 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 85min, 2nd: 121min, 3rd: 157min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 100km waypoint, 7 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 95 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 80min, 3rd: 113min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of relief supply box (66kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 60km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 76 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 76min, 2nd: 109min, 3rd: 142min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=312km, boat still-water speed=26km/h, departure=9:00 AM
  Current: 7km/h (downstream in Zone B)
  Cargo: 66kg relief supply boxx15 + 24kg water barrelx6

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 351km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 9 units of relief supply box (68kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 230km waypoint, 9 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 105 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 75 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 75min, 2nd: 108min, 3rd: 141min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 304km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of water barrel (19kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (34kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 210km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 88min, 3rd: 119min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=304km, boat still-water speed=31km/h, departure=7:00 AM
  Current: 7km/h (downstream in Zone A)
  Cargo: 19kg water barrelx18 + 34kg relief supply boxx6
  Congestion: 8:00 AM~1:00 PM, Zone B -37%
  Mid-journey delivery: 210km, water barrel x10
  Cumulative fatigue: +31min per rest

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 330km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 20 units of water barrel (17kg each) and 6 units of construction material (52kg each). The boat can travel at 21km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 41km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 280km waypoint, 13 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 100 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 82min, 3rd: 116min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 300km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (70kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 216km waypoint, 7 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 83 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 83min, 2nd: 113min, 3rd: 143min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=300km, boat still-water speed=24km/h, departure=7:00 AM
  Current: 8km/h (downstream in Zone B)
  Cargo: 6kg medical kitx13 + 70kg relief supply boxx8
  Congestion: 9:00 AM~3:00 PM, Zone B -32%
  Mid-journey delivery: 216km, medical kit x7
  Cumulative fatigue: +30min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 24, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 19, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 14}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 115, 'duration_minutes': 83, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 9}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 200, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 15, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 38}}
[STEP 1] Cargo weight: (6x13)+(70x8)=638kg
[STEP 2] Cargo regulation: 638kg > 200kg -> Zone A -15%(16.15), Zone B -38%(8.68)
[STEP 3] Zone A (0.0km, 7.00h): 24-8=16.0, limit 16.15 -> 16.00km/h

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 348km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 9 units of construction material (63kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 150km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 72 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 72min, 2nd: 110min, 3rd: 148min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 304km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (58kg each) and 9 units of water barrel (25kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.

From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 90km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 68 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 68min, 2nd: 96min, 3rd: 124min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=304km, boat still-water speed=32km/h, departure=9:00 AM
  Current: 8km/h (downstream in Zone B)
  Cargo: 58kg construction materialx11 + 25kg water barrelx9
  Congestion: 11:00 AM~5:00 PM, Zone B -38%
  Mid-journey delivery: 90km, construction material x5
  Cumulative fatigue: +28min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 23, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 23, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 15}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 108, 'duration_minutes': 68, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 9}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 400, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 22, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 38}}
[STEP 1] Cargo weight: (58x11)+(25x9)=863kg
[STEP 2] Cargo regulation: 863kg > 400kg -> Zone A -22%(17.94), Zone B -38%(9.30)
[STEP 3] Zone A (0.0km, 9.00h): 32-8=24.0, limit 17.94 -> 17.94km/h
  -> 9.00km / 17.94km/h = 0.5017h
[STEP 4] Zone A (9.0km, 9.50h): 32-8=24.0, limit 17.94 -> 17.94km/h
  -> 9.00km / 17.94km/h = 0.5017h
[STEP 5] Zone A (18.0km, 10.00h): 32-8=24.0, limit 17.94 -> 17.94km/h
  -> 5.00km / 17.94km/h = 0.2787h
[STEP 6] Zone B (23.0km, 10.28h): 32+8=40.0, limit 9.30 -> 9.30km/h

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 319km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 19 units of relief supply box (52kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 99km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 61 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 61min, 2nd: 91min, 3rd: 121min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 323km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 9 units of relief supply box (66kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 43km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 150km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 90min, 3rd: 120min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 299km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (36kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (16kg each). The boat can travel at 22km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 270km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 61 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 61min, 2nd: 91min, 3rd: 121min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 19 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 58 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 58min, 2nd: 88min, 3rd: 118min, ...).

Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 322km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 22 units of medical kit (12kg each) and 8 units of construction material (78kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 260km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 84 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 84min, 2nd: 116min, 3rd: 148min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 303km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 110km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 78 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 78min, 2nd: 115min, 3rd: 152min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 110 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 75min, 3rd: 102min, ...).

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 180km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 100 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 86min, 3rd: 119min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 339km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 8 units of construction material (62kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 160km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 59 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 59min, 2nd: 87min, 3rd: 115min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 334km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (22kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (41kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 70km waypoint, 7 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 68 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 68min, 2nd: 95min, 3rd: 122min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=334km, boat still-water speed=24km/h, departure=8:00 AM
  Current: 7km/h (downstream in Zone B)
  Cargo: 22kg water barrelx13 + 41kg relief supply boxx10
  Congestion: 10:00 AM~3:00 PM, Zone B -39%
  Mid-journey delivery: 70km, water barrel x7
  Cumulative fatigue: +27min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 23, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 19, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 14}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 108, 'duration_minutes': 68, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 10}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 400, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 22, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 32}}
[STEP 1] Cargo weight: (22x13)+(41x10)=696kg
[STEP 2] Cargo regulation: 696kg > 400kg -> Zone A -22%(14.82), Zone B -32%(9.52)

  -> 10.00km / 14.82km/h = 0.6748h

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 366km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 20 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 4 units of construction material (58kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 100km waypoint, 12 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 120 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 78 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 78min, 2nd: 108min, 3rd: 138min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 339km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (22kg each) and 7 units of construction material (50kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 44km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 55km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 117 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 59 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 59min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 129min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:
[STEP 0] Initial conditions
  Total distance=339km, boat still-water speed=36km/h, departure=8:00 AM
  Current: 8km/h (downstream in Zone A)
  Cargo: 22kg water barrelx12 + 50kg construction materialx7
  Congestion: 10:00 AM~4:00 PM, Zone B -38%
  Mid-journey delivery: 55km, construction material x2
  Cumulative fatigue: +35min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 44, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 28, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 14}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 117, 'duration_minutes': 59, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 11}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 400, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 19, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 34}}

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 20 units of water barrel (16kg each) and 7 units of construction material (51kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 43km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.

From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 120km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 119 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 82min, 3rd: 118min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 299km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (79kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (56kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 130km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 82 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 82min, 2nd: 119min, 3rd: 156min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 9 units of relief supply box (46kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.

From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 242km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 104 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 98min, 3rd: 133min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 310km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 6 units of construction material (77kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 250km waypoint, 6 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 76 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 76min, 2nd: 107min, 3rd: 138min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 347km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of water barrel (16kg each) and 10 units of construction material (66kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 220km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 83min, 3rd: 117min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 325km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (60kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 43km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 126km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 110 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 72 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 72min, 2nd: 103min, 3rd: 134min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 21 units of medical kit (7kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (51kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 190km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 74 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 74min, 2nd: 104min, 3rd: 134min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 307km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (72kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 210km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 65 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 65min, 2nd: 99min, 3rd: 133min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (39kg each). The boat can travel at 22km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 41km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 162km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 105 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 87min, 3rd: 117min, ...).

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 329km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of medical kit (10kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (51kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.

From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 253km waypoint, 10 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 118 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 85min, 3rd: 119min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (57kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (25kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 207km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 102 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 72 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 72min, 2nd: 101min, 3rd: 130min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 300km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (41kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (23kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 120km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 113 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 55 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 55min, 2nd: 84min, 3rd: 113min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 330km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (60kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 120km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 75min, 3rd: 103min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (41kg each) and 9 units of construction material (71kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 120 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 59 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 59min, 2nd: 88min, 3rd: 117min, ...).

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (74kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (22kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 99 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 80 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 80min, 2nd: 116min, 3rd: 152min, ...).

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 343km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of water barrel (15kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (70kg each). The boat can travel at 22km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 250km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 78min, 3rd: 109min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (61kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (21kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.

At the 88km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 68 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 68min, 2nd: 96min, 3rd: 124min, ...).

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (54kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (64kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 20 units of water barrel (18kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (65kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 230km waypoint, 9 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 62 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 62min, 2nd: 96min, 3rd: 130min, ...).

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 345km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of medical kit (10kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (56kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 260km waypoint, 10 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 111 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 70 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 70min, 2nd: 106min, 3rd: 142min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
