He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (51kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.

From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 290km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 77 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 77min, 2nd: 105min, 3rd: 133min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

  Total distance=328km, boat still-water speed=26km/h, departure=9:00 AM

  Cargo: 51kg construction materialx12 + 17kg water barrelx6
  Congestion: 10:00 AM~4:00 PM, Zone B -34%
  Mid-journey delivery: 290km, water barrel x2
  Cumulative fatigue: +28min per rest
  Regulations: {'speed_limit': {'zone_A_km': 34, 'zone_A_limit_kph': 20, 'zone_B_limit_kph': 16}, 'mandatory_rest': {'trigger_minutes': 107, 'duration_minutes': 77, 'rest_stop_interval_km': 10}, 'cargo_effect': {'heavy_load_kg': 400, 'zone_A_reduction_percent': 23, 'zone_B_reduction_percent': 32}}

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 347km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 22 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (45kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.

From 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 220km waypoint, 14 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 73 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 73min, 2nd: 105min, 3rd: 137min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 349km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 19 units of water barrel (18kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (46kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 36km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 76min, 3rd: 107min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 328km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 22 units of construction material (50kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (56kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 63km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 104 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 88min, 3rd: 123min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 316km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 14 units of medical kit (10kg each) and 9 units of water barrel (21kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 300km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 338km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (68kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (40kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.

From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 187km waypoint, 9 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 110 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 55 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 55min, 2nd: 90min, 3rd: 125min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
